metadata.summary: Viral loop optimization iterates on an existing measured K-factor by decomposing it into its two drivers — i (invites sent per user) and c (invitee conversion rate) — identifying the bottleneck, and running A/B tests on the weakest leg. Optimize c first (invite landing page) before optimizing i (share moments); every improvement to c multiplies all future loop turns.

content.title: Viral Loop Optimization
content.summary: Viral loop optimization iterates on an existing measured K-factor by decomposing it into its two drivers — i (invites sent per user) and c (invitee conversion rate) — identifying the bottleneck, and running A/B tests on the weakest leg. Optimize c first (invite landing page) before optimizing i (share moments); every improvement to c multiplies all future loop turns.

why.p: K-factor is a compound metric: K = i * c. A small improvement to the lower leg often has larger absolute impact than a large improvement to the higher one. Without funnel decomposition, optimization effort is directed by intuition rather than math. Cycle time is a secondary lever — halving cycle time roughly squares compounding over a quarter.

list.item: Product has a working invite/share path and a measured baseline K (even K = 0.05 is enough to start).
Full event chain is tracked: invite_shown → invite_sent → invite_clicked → invitee_signup → invitee_active.
A live A/B test framework is available (no code deploy needed to split variants).
At least 5,000 weekly users entering the loop (otherwise sample size kills experiments).
Pre-PMF: optimizing K of a product nobody loves yields K = 0.0X times any multiplier — still nothing.
B2B enterprise where sharing is gated by procurement or security — viral mechanics do not translate.
Markets with strict anti-spam rules (regulated finance, EU consumer) where contact-import is non-compliant.
Products where the inviter's value requires the invitee to join but the invitee has no standalone reason — forced virality churns invitees fast.

rule.statement: Decompose K into i (avg invites sent per user) and c (invitee conversion rate = signups / invites sent) before deciding where to optimize; optimize c first.
rule.rationale: Improving c lifts every future loop turn because all future invites benefit from higher conversion; improving i only increases volume into the current conversion rate.

list.item: K = i * c
i = total invite_sent events / distinct active users in period
c = invitee_signup events matched to invite tokens / total invite_sent events
cycle_time = median days between invite_sent and invitee_signup

rule.statement: Measure cycle time (p50 days from invite_sent to invitee_signup) and treat it as a primary optimization lever alongside i and c.
rule.rationale: K = 0.4 with 3-day cycle time beats K = 0.7 with 60-day cycle time over a quarter in compounding terms.

list.item: reduce cycle time by: one-click mobile signup, pre-filled email, social login for invitees
track cycle_time weekly; a sudden increase signals friction introduced in a signup change
invite_shown: % of active users who see the invite prompt
invite_sent: % of users who actually send an invite (= share rate)
invite_clicked: clicks on the invite link by recipients
invitee_signup: recipients who complete signup
invitee_active: invitees who reach the activation milestone within D7

rule.statement: Track invitee D30 retention separately from organic D30; if invitee retention is <50% of organic, the loop is poisoning the cohort.
rule.rationale: Incentivized loops attract users who join for the reward, not the product; high K with low invitee retention grows the user count but collapses LTV.

rule.statement: Place the share prompt at peak-emotion moments (just completed a milestone, just discovered something great) — not in settings menus.
rule.rationale: Share prompts in settings rely on users seeking them out; achievement-moment prompts capture naturally high intent and produce 3–5x higher share rates.

list.item: achievement trigger (completed goal, hit milestone) → celebration modal with share button
discovery trigger (found something valuable) → "share with a friend who'd love this"
social trigger (want to connect) → "invite teammates to collaborate"
help trigger (need someone else to use it) → collaborative feature invite
achievement triggers — easy / high impact
multi-channel sharing (email, SMS, copy link) — medium / medium
viral content (shareable badges, results images) — medium / high
contact import (bulk invite) — hard / high (privacy constraints; iOS/Android blocked post-2021)
team features that require collaboration — hard / very high

rule.statement: Personal invites ("Anna invited you to [Product]") beat brand invites by 20–40% on click and signup; implement with a single DB join on the referral token.
rule.rationale: Named social proof reduces perceived spam risk and creates accountability, increasing click-through before the landing page is even reached.

list.item: personalize invite landing: show inviter name + avatar + their activity context
add social proof: "join N people using [Product]"
one-click signup: Google/Apple SSO, pre-filled email from invite token
show value before signup: preview of what the invitee will access
mobile-optimize: reduce tap count to ≤3 from invite link to activated account

antipattern.description: Requiring signup to view shared content.
antipattern.reason: Forcing pre-signup registration drops invitee conversion by 40–60%; a preview wall converts better than a hard gate.

rule.statement: Reward at value-realization (invitee reaches activation milestone), not at signup, to cut incentive fraud.
rule.rationale: Signup-triggered rewards are easy to game with fake accounts; activation-triggered rewards require genuine engagement that fraud bots cannot mimic at scale.

list.item: one-time reward: $10 credit after invitee activates
tiered multi-referral: 1 referral → $10, 5 → $50 + badge, 10 → $100 + premium feature
two-sided: both inviter and invitee receive reward — higher acceptance rate

rule.statement: Instrument a fraud-checker subagent to review referrer-IP / device-graph anomalies before any reward is issued on a new incentive structure.
rule.rationale: Incentivized loops attract self-referrers and bot signups; raw K looks great while real LTV plummets — "optimizing" without fraud feedback maximizes gameable referrals.

list.item: self-referral: inviter and invitee share the same IP, device fingerprint, or email domain
bot signups: invitee accounts with zero post-signup events within 24h
reward farming: single user accumulates >10% of all rewards in the period
velocity anomaly: single referrer sends 10x the median invite rate
invitee D7 retention vs organic D7 retention — flag if invitee is <50% of organic
invitee LTV vs organic LTV at D90 — if <60%, incentive is attracting wrong users
invitee-to-inviter conversion: what % of referred users also invite someone? — secondary K

rule.statement: Anchor K-factor expectations to category benchmarks, not to the README's case study — consumer apps: K=0.1–0.3; B2B SaaS: K=0.05–0.2.
rule.rationale: Agents over-cite Dropbox/Spotify Wrapped as if any product can replicate them; unrealistic K targets lead to dark-pattern share mechanics that backfire.

list.item: weekly: check K-factor trend, invite funnel drop-offs, top referrers, plan next experiment
monthly: cohort analysis of signups by source, retention of referred vs organic, fraud check, channel performance
quarterly: review loop type effectiveness, major product change to improve virality, incentive structure, competitive analysis

rule.statement: Redesign viral loop mechanics every 12–18 months; users learn the pattern and c decays as familiarity reduces novelty.
rule.rationale: "Personalized invite" wins early then plateaus; design for refresh, not permanence.

template.purpose: K-factor decomposition from event stream CSV
Naive compounding model for K + cycle time projection
K-factor experiment spec template (hypothesis, primary metric, MDE, variants)
